text_clean: I was absolutely horrible at math. I couldn't understand the abstract. I could never remember which formula went with what. When I was in school there was no special education, but I knew I couldn't learn what they were teaching me. It made no sense. I was so ashamed. But years later when I was a single mother and going to college I had one math teacher that failed me because I could not attend tutoring when he had it, but he was also Indian and I couldn't understand him anyway. I decided to retake the course in the summer and had an Egyptian teacher. He started at the beginning built up and gave us a test, he then went back two steps, showed the connection and built it back up and gave a test. It was that magic light bulb that went on!!!!!!! IT MADE SENSE!!!!!!!!!!!!!. I ended the semester with an A and I teach the way he taught because I concretely could see the connection. That is why I became a SPED teacher!
text_rewritten: I'm sorry to say that I was terrible at math, I couldn't even remember which formula to use, and there was no special education at all. It made me feel like I couldn't learn anything, especially since I couldn't afford to go to college. Eventually, years later, I had to turn to one of my math teachers, who failed me because I couldn't attend a Tufts tutor.

text_clean: I so agree with your focus points! I wonder if some of our technology is to blame for students attitudes? So many times I hear about how technology will do it for them. They really believe that. It is frustrating to see students in 7th and 8th grade who lack a basic fundamental understanding from elementary math and lack calculator skills; they don't see it as the root of their problems in my class. It is like they throw up their hands and say \Someone or something else will solve it for me.\""
